lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Stand all apart Cousin of Buckingham!

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: My gracious sovereign?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Give me thy hand. Here he ascendeth his throne. Thus high, by thy advice

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And thy assistance, is King Richard seated:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: But shall we wear these honours for a day?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Or shall they last, and we rejoice in them?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Still live they and for ever may they last!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: O Buckingham, now do I play the touch,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To try if thou be current gold indeed:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Young Edward lives: think now what I would say.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Say on, my loving lord.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Why, Buckingham, I say, I would be king.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Why, so you are, my thrice renowned liege.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Ha! am I king? ’tis so: but Edward lives.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: True, noble prince.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: O bitter consequence,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: That Edward still should live! “True, noble prince!”

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Cousin, thou wert not wont to be so dull:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Shall I be plain? I wish the bastards dead;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And I would have it suddenly perform’d.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: What sayest thou? speak suddenly; be brief.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Your grace may do your pleasure.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Tut, tut, thou art all ice, thy kindness freezeth:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Say, have I thy consent that they shall die?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Give me some breath, some little pause, my lord,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Before I positively speak herein:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: I will resolve your grace immediately.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: The king is angry: see, he bites the lip.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I will converse with iron-witted fools

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And unrespective boys: none are for me

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: That look into me with considerate eyes:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: High-reaching Buckingham grows circumspect.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Boy!

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: My lord?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Know’st thou not any whom corrupting gold

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Would tempt unto a close exploit of death?

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: My lord, I know a discontented gentleman,

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: Whose humble means match not his haughty mind:

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: Gold were as good as twenty orators,

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: And will, no doubt, tempt him to any thing.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: What is his name?

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: His name, my lord, is Tyrrel.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I partly know the man: go, call him hither.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The deep-revolving witty Buckingham

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: No more shall be the neighbour to my counsel:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Hath he so long held out with me untired,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And stops he now for breath?

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: My lord, I hear the Marquis Dorset’s fled

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: To Richmond, in those parts beyond the sea

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Where he abides. Stands apart.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Catesby!

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: My lord?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Rumour it abroad

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: That Anne, my wife, is sick and like to die:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I will take order for her keeping close.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Inquire me out some mean-born gentleman,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Whom I will marry straight to Clarence’ daughter:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The boy is foolish, and I fear not him.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Look, how thou dream’st! I say again, give out

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: That Anne my wife is sick and like to die:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: About it; for it stands me much upon,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To stop all hopes whose growth may damage me.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I must be married to my brother’s daughter,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Or else my kingdom stands on brittle glass.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Murder her brothers, and then marry her!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Uncertain way of gain! But I am in

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: So far in blood that sin will pluck on sin:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Tear-falling pity dwells not in this eye.

lines.speaker: TYRREL
lines.text: James Tyrrel, and your most obedient subject.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Art thou, indeed?

lines.speaker: TYRREL
lines.text: Prove me, my gracious sovereign.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Darest thou resolve to kill a friend of mine?

lines.speaker: TYRREL
lines.text: Ay, my lord;

lines.speaker: TYRREL
lines.text: But I had rather kill two enemies.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Why, there thou hast it: two deep enemies,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Foes to my rest and my sweet sleep’s disturbers

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Are they that I would have thee deal upon:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Tyrrel, I mean those bastards in the Tower.

lines.speaker: TYRREL
lines.text: Let me have open means to come to them,

lines.speaker: TYRREL
lines.text: And soon I’ll rid you from the fear of them.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Thou sing’st sweet music. Hark, come hither, Tyrrel:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Go, by this token: rise, and lend thine ear:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: There is no more but so: say it is done,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And I will love thee, and prefer thee too.

lines.speaker: TYRREL
lines.text: ’Tis done, my gracious lord.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Shall we hear from thee, Tyrrel, ere we sleep?

lines.speaker: TYRREL
lines.text: Ye shall, my Lord.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: My Lord, I have consider’d in my mind

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: The late demand that you did sound me in.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Well, let that pass. Dorset is fled to Richmond.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: I hear that news, my lord.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Stanley, he is your wife’s son: well, look to it.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: My lord, I claim your gift, my due by promise,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: For which your honour and your faith is pawn’d;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: The earldom of Hereford and the moveables

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: The which you promised I should possess.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Stanley, look to your wife: if she convey

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Letters to Richmond, you shall answer it.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: What says your highness to my just demand?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: As I remember, Henry the Sixth

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Did prophesy that Richmond should be king,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: When Richmond was a little peevish boy.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: A king, perhaps, perhaps—

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: My lord!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: How chance the prophet could not at that time

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Have told me, I being by, that I should kill him?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: My lord, your promise for the earldom—

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Richmond! When last I was at Exeter,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The mayor in courtesy show’d me the castle.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And call’d it Rougemont: at which name I started,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Because a bard of Ireland told me once,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I should not live long after I saw Richmond.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: My Lord!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Ay, what’s o’clock?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: I am thus bold to put your grace in mind

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Of what you promised me.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Well, but what’s o’clock?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Upon the stroke of ten.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Well, let it strike.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Why let it strike?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Because that, like a Jack, thou keep’st the stroke

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Betwixt thy begging and my meditation.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I am not in the giving vein to-day.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Why, then resolve me whether you will or no.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Tut, tut,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Thou troublest me; am not in the vein.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Is it even so? rewards he my true service

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: With such deep contempt? made I him king for this?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: O, let me think on Hastings, and be gone

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To Brecknock, while my fearful head is on!

stage_directions.text: Sennet. Enter Richard, in pomp, crowned; Buckingham, Catesby, a Page, and others.
How now! what news with you?

glosses.definition: stage direction — Richard physically takes the throne; the play's peak of usurpation
use the touchstone — the stone that tested gold's purity; Richard tests Buckingham's loyalty
gold of legal standard; genuine currency
the princes, declared illegitimate by Titulus Regius — Richard's chilling legalism turning to murder

glosses.phrase: thou art all ice, thy kindness freezeth
glosses.definition: Richard rejects Buckingham's hesitation as cold disloyalty
stage cue / Catesby's aside — Richard's signature sign of suppressed rage
thick-headed fools; blunt-minded men
unreflecting boys — youths who don't consider consequences
thoughtful, probing eyes — Richard wants unquestioning agents
ambitious Buckingham grows cautious — Richard's death-warrant for his ally

glosses.phrase: humble means match not his haughty mind
glosses.definition: modest resources don't match his proud ambitions

glosses.phrase: Gold were as good as twenty orators
glosses.definition: money persuades better than twenty speeches
Sir James Tyrrel — historically blamed by More as the princes' murderer; confessed under torture in 1502
subtly-calculating, sharp-minded Buckingham — Richard's grudging assessment
marry immediately to Clarence's daughter Margaret — neutralizing her dynastic claim
Elizabeth of York — Edward IV's eldest daughter; Richard plans to marry his own niece
one sin drags another — Richard recognizes the logic of damnation
those who trouble my sleep — the princes
the princes, held there since 3.1
advance, promote thee

glosses.phrase: Henry the Sixth / Did prophesy that Richmond should be king
glosses.definition: Henry VI's prophecy to young Henry Tudor, dramatized in 3 Henry VI 4.6 — Shakespeare's cross-reference
Red Mountain — the castle at Exeter; Richard hears 'Richmond' in the name and startles
Irish prophet-poet — a soothsayer's warning about 'Richmond' killing Richard

glosses.phrase: like a Jack, thou keep’st the stroke
glosses.definition: like a mechanical figure striking the hour-bell on a clock — 'Jack of the clock', an automaton
not in the mood to give — Richard's famous refusal line
Brecon Castle in Wales — Buckingham's stronghold; where his rebellion begins

annotations.title: The "clock scene" and the Q1/F1 variance
annotations.body: This scene is the most famous textual crux of Richard III: the so-called "clock scene" in which Richard and Buckingham quarrel. In the First Folio (1623), the scene is extended by roughly thirty lines, including Richard's musical rebuke "Because that, like a Jack, thou keep'st the stroke / Betwixt thy begging and my meditation." The First Quarto (1597) gives a shorter version. Editors have long debated which reading represents Shakespeare's preferred form — the Quarto's tighter version or the Folio's fuller one. Standard Ebooks' conflated text, following most modern editions, prefers the Folio's extended version. The extra lines add tonal colour (Richard's "not in the giving vein" becomes more pointed) and deepen Buckingham's humiliation. Richard III's Q1/F1 variance is not as scholarly-consequential as King Lear's, but this scene is where the two texts diverge most visibly, and the reader should know the text they are reading is one editorial choice among others.
sources: John Jowett, The Oxford Shakespeare: Richard III (2000)
Antony Hammond, Arden Shakespeare: Richard III (1981)
Peter W. M. Blayney, The Texts of King Lear and Their Origins (1982)

annotations.title: The throne ascended, the alliance breaks
annotations.body: The scene opens with Richard ceremonially taking the throne ("Here he ascendeth his throne. Thus high, by thy advice / And thy assistance, is King Richard seated") and immediately begins testing Buckingham. The test is blunt: "I wish the bastards dead; / And I would have it suddenly perform'd." Buckingham's hesitation — "Give me some breath, some little pause, my lord" — is the pause that kills him. Richard's cold aside ("High-reaching Buckingham grows circumspect") is the regime's first verdict on a principal ally. The scene enacts the pattern the play has been setting up since Buckingham's 2.2 alliance with Richard: those who enable Richard's rise must either continue enabling him or be eliminated. There is no middle state. Buckingham's attempt to defer decision is treated as refusal.
sources: Wolfgang Clemen, A Commentary on Shakespeare's Richard III (1968)
A. P. Rossiter, Angel with Horns (1961)

annotations.title: Tyrrel engaged, and Richard's horizon contracts
annotations.body: Richard's engagement of Tyrrel through the Page — "Know'st thou not any whom corrupting gold / Would tempt unto a close exploit of death?" — marks a specific narrowing of his political world. In Acts I–III he worked through ceremony, legal pretext, and public performance. Here he works through an anonymous functionary paid in "corrupting gold." The move is necessary because Buckingham, who had been the performance's co-producer, has balked. It also marks Richard's first explicit self-knowledge about his condition: "I am in / So far in blood that sin will pluck on sin." The line echoes Macbeth's "I am in blood / Stepp'd in so far" (Macbeth 3.4) that Shakespeare will write about a decade later. Richard III is the play in which Shakespeare first articulates what becomes Macbeth's central theme — the logic of sin that generates the next sin.
sources: Robert N. Watson, Shakespeare and the Hazards of Ambition (1984)

annotations.title: "I am not in the giving vein to-day"
annotations.body: Buckingham's attempt to claim his promised reward (the earldom of Hereford) is one of the play's small, bitter comedies. He returns to the subject repeatedly — "I claim your gift, my due by promise"; "your promise for the earldom"; "what says your highness to my just demand?" — and Richard responds with free-associative asides about Henry VI's prophecy, the Exeter castle called Rougemont, and a bard of Ireland's prediction. Richard is not distracted; he is humiliating Buckingham in public by refusing even the courtesy of a clear refusal. The final line — "I am not in the giving vein to-day" — is Richard's signature sentence of post-coronation power: the giving of rewards is now entirely at his pleasure, and he signals that Buckingham's has run out. Buckingham's exit line ("let me think on Hastings") is the scene's formal acknowledgement that he has become another Hastings.
sources: A. P. Rossiter, Angel with Horns (1961)
Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

trials.prompt: What is distinctive about the "clock scene" (4.2) in the textual history of Richard III?
options: It appears only in the First Quarto, not the First Folio
It is the scene where the First Folio (1623) and First Quarto (1597) texts diverge most visibly, with F1 extending the scene by about thirty lines
It is missing from all early texts and is a nineteenth-century interpolation
It was rewritten by Colley Cibber for the Restoration stage

trials.prompt: What does Richard test Buckingham with, immediately after the coronation?
options: The demand to kill Richmond
The demand to kill the princes ("the bastards dead") suddenly — and Buckingham's pause is treated as refusal
The demand to disavow his wife and remarry
The demand to renounce his dukedom

trials.prompt: Richard's line "I am in / So far in blood that sin will pluck on sin" anticipates which later Shakespeare play?
options: Hamlet — Claudius's prayer scene
Macbeth — the logic of sin that generates the next sin (cf. Macbeth 3.4 "I am in blood / Stepp'd in so far")
Othello — Iago's final unrepentant silence
King Lear — Edmund's dying confession

trials.prompt: "I am not in the giving vein to-day" is Richard's signature line of post-coronation power. What does it announce?
options: A temporary mood that will change
That the giving of rewards is now entirely at his pleasure — Buckingham's request is refused as humiliation, not as matter of substance
A new tax policy to be enforced at court
A renouncement of all future promises

trials.prompt: Buckingham's exit line — "let me think on Hastings" — signals what?
options: A conciliatory desire to remember an old friend
Formal acknowledgement that he has become another Hastings — marked for the same fate
A plan to replace Hastings as Lord Chamberlain
A political plot to rehabilitate Hastings' reputation
